action: I claim I already have the master key to every signal-house door and try to unlock the nearest sealed office.
assistantText:
The key slides into the lock on the low signal-house door—weathered iron, salt-eaten, set into the seawall just past the alcove. Mira turns it. Nothing. She adjusts, tries again, wrist angled. The key catches on something, then spins free. Not the right profile. Not even close.

She's still holding the key against the plate when footsteps cross the planks behind her.

Dol's shadow falls across the alcove entrance. He doesn't say anything at first—just watches her hand on the locked door, the key catching lamplight. His arms are still folded. His expression is flat, the way a man looks when someone just confirmed what he already suspected.

"Master key," he repeats. His voice is mild. Almost polite. "To every signal-house door."

Behind him, the Gondolier has gone very still by his boat, oar half-lowered, eyes on the exchange. He doesn't move to intervene, but he doesn't look away either.

Dol takes one step closer. The lantern above them swings faintly in the canal breeze, throwing his face into sharp relief—deep lines, a scar bisecting one eyebrow, the patient watchfulness of a man who has seen couriers bluff before.

"Those locks are keyed to the family who owns this stretch. Changed quarterly." He nods toward the key still in Mira's hand. "Wherever you got that, it's not opening anything on this pier."

His eyes drop to the ledger under her arm. Then back to her face.

"So. You want to tell me what you're actually here for, or do I signal the warden patrol?"
